title: Cannot read container mount files
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I'm trying to use postgres in a podman container with a custom mounted data directory. I'm able to create the container and the database works, but I lose the ability to read any files in the database data folder from the launching user account.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `mkdir data`

2. `podman run --publish 5432 -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=password -v ./data:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z -it --rm postgres:13`

**Describe the results you received:**

Running `ls data` gives a permissions denied, but running `sudo ls -l data` shows that all the files inside were created with 100998:100998, and have `drwx` permissions.

**Describe the results you expected:**

With postgres running the processes as non-root, this is the result I expect.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

So far I've tried setting `--user root` and other combinations to attempt to get the container to run the postgres process as my user ID, but nothing seems to work and I'm at a loss as to how this is supposed to operate.

The following seem to be related to this:
* https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/rootless-podman-makes-sense
* https://github.com/docker-library/postgres/pull/253

The article seems very close, but in reverse (unable to write inside the image).

**Output of `podman version`:**

`podman version 2.1.1`

comments.body:
What user is the application in the container running as? It doesn't seem to be running as root, if you're seeing files created as a UID not equal to the run that ran Podman.

One option would be to use `--userns=keep-id` and run the app in the container as the same ID as your user. The `--userns=keep-id` flag ensures that your UID inside the container is mapped to the same UID outside the container so there should be no permissions issues there. This could be a matter of using the `--user` flag, or possibly that image drops privileges and runs as a non-root user (you'll need to consult documentation for the image in that case)

I believe the server in the container is running as the `postgres` user.

Attempting what you suggested:
```
$ podman run --rm -it -v ./data:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z --user $USER --userns=keep-id postgres:13
Error: unable to find user zeranoe: no matching entries in passwd file
```
You'll need to either add your user to the container, or use your UID instead of username as an argument to `--user`

Thank you so much! The following worked:
`podman run --rm -it -v ./data:/var/lib/postgresql/data:Z --user $(id -u) -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=pw --userns=keep-id postgres:13`
